source_metadata.title_raw: William M. Carroll - A-19-067 - August 19, 2019 - Yreka

content.full_text:
STATE OF CALIFORNIA
FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION
1102 Q Street • Suite 3000 • Sacramento, CA 95811
(916) 322-5660 • Fax (916) 322-0886

August 19, 2019

William M. Carroll
Deputy County Counsel
Office of the County Counsel
County of Siskiyou
P.O. Box 659
Yreka, CA 96097

Re: Your Request for Advice
Our File No. A-19-067

Dear Mr. Carroll:

This letter responds to your request for advice on behalf of Siskiyou County Planning
Commissioner Jeff Fowle regarding the conflict of interest provisions of the Political Reform Act
(the "Act").¹ Please note that we are only providing advice under the conflict of interest provisions
of the Act and not under any other conflict of interest laws. Also note that we are not a finder of fact
when considering advice (In re Oglesby (1975) 1 FPPC Ops. 71), and any advice we provide assumes
your facts are complete and accurate. If this is not the case or if the facts underlying these decisions
should change, you should contact us for additional advice.

QUESTIONS

(1) Does the Act prohibit Commissioner Fowle from taking part in governmental decisions
relating to:

(A) A proposed "Agritourism Zoning Ordinance," which would expressly authorize
additional uses of real property parcels of 10 acres or more zoned Rural Residential, Prime
Agricultural, or Non-Prime Agricultural given that the Commissioner's agricultural business
is currently engaged in some of those uses on the Commissioner's real property zoned Prime
Agricultural or Non-Prime Agricultural?

(B) A proposed "Multispecies Zoning Ordinance" which would authorize "youth
agricultural projects" and "actively managed pasture grazing of hogs or poultry" on real
property zoned Rural Residential, Prime Agricultural, or Non-Prime Agricultural?

¹ The Political Reform Act is contained in Government Code Sections 81000 through 91014. All statutory
references are to the Government Code, unless otherwise indicated. The regulations of the Fair Political Practices
Commission are contained in Sections 18110 through 18997 of Title 2 of the California Code of Regulations. All
regulatory references are to Title 2, Division 6 of the California Code of Regulations, unless otherwise indicated.

File No. A-19-067
Page No. 2

(2) If the Commissioner is disqualified from decisions relating to either the Agritourism
Zoning Ordinance or Multispecies Zoning Ordinance under the Act, may the Planning Commission
invoke the Legally Required Participation Exception in order to take action on the Ordinance?

CONCLUSIONS

(1)(A) Yes. The Act prohibits the Commissioner from taking part in decisions relating to the
Agritourism Zoning Ordinance because it is reasonably foreseeable that those decisions would have
a material financial effect on the Commissioner's interest in his agricultural business based on the
facts presented.

(1)(B) No. The Act would not prohibit the Commissioner from taking part in decisions
relating to the Multispecies Zoning Ordinance because it is not reasonably foreseeable that those
decisions would have a material financial effect on the value of one or more of the Commissioner's
parcels zoned Prime Agricultural or Non-Prime Agricultural based on the facts presented.

(2) Yes. The Planning Commission may invoke the Legally Required Participation
Exception in order to take action on the Agritourism Zoning Ordinance if it is unable to form a
quorum of members who are not disqualified from those decisions.

FACTS AS PRESENTED BY REQUESTER

You are a Deputy County Counsel for the County of Siskiyou and the authorized
representative of Planning Commissioner Jeff Fowle. The Planning Commission will soon consider
two separate resolutions, one recommending that the Board of Supervisors enact a proposed
"Agritourism Zoning Ordinance" (the "AZO"), and the other recommending that the Board of
Supervisors enact a proposed "Multispecies Ordinance" (the "MZO"). Both the AZO and the MZO
would authorize additional uses of real property parcels within the County zoned Rural Residential,
Prime Agricultural, or Non-Prime Agricultural (collectively, the "RR/AG Parcels").

The AZO would authorize an owner of an RR/AG Parcel of 10 acres or more to hold as
many as 20 single-day events per year with a maximum of 30 guests per event, and as many as
three single-day events with a maximum of 150 guests per event, on that RR/AG Parcel. The AZO
would also authorize a RR/AG Parcel owner, upon obtaining an administrative use permit from the County, to hold twice as many single-day events on his or her
RR/AG Parcel as authorized without such a permit; hold fruit and vegetable picking and sales; hold
on-site sales of agricultural products related to the farm; and to offer on-site "Farmstays," which
you describe as essentially an agricultural bed and breakfast," and "agritourism camping," which
you describe as "transitory overnight occupancy to facilitate engagement in agritourism."

The MZO would authorize an owner of an RR/AG Parcel to engage in "actively managed
pasture grazing of hogs and poultry," and to conduct "youth agricultural projects, such as 4-H
projects raising livestock, on the RR/AG Parcel.

The Commissioner owns five RR/AG Parcels within the County, each of which is zoned
Prime Agricultural or Non-Prime Agricultural. Together, these five parcels consist of approximately
503 acres, and are worth more than $2,000. The Commissioner operates an agricultural business

File No. A-19-067
Page No. 3

from those RR/AG Parcels which engages in on-site direct sales of hay, cattle and beef, and
provides on-site horse-training activities, including free overnight stays associated with those
activities. The Commissioner's family has been pursuing these uses for 50 years or more.

The Commissioner's agricultural business's annual gross revenues are approximately
$530,000 to $550,000. You state that it is theoretically possible that the Commissioner's
agricultural business could achieve an additional five percent of its current annual gross income
through the addition of an agritourist use on his RR/AG Parcels pursuant to the AZO if enacted.
While the Commissioner's RR/AG Parcel is theoretically able to accommodate a commercial
pasture grazing operation, the transition to such an operation from his existing agricultural business
would be a major change.

Because both the AZO and MZO authorize additional uses of RR/AG Parcels, you
conservatively assume enactment of either would result in some slight increase in the market value
of the Commissioner's RR/AG Parcels. If enacted, the AZO would apply to 1,734 RR/AG Parcels,
totaling 3,457,669 acres. The MZO would apply to 2,457 RR/AG Parcels, totaling approximately
3,460,673 acres. There are 57,183 parcels, totaling 4,017,849.6 total acres, within the County.

ANALYSIS

The Act's conflict of interest provisions prohibit a public official from taking part in a
governmental decision if it is reasonably foreseeable that the decision would have a material
financial effect on one or more of the official's financial interests distinguishable from its effect on
the public generally. (Sections 87100 and 87103.) An official's interests that may give rise to a
disqualifying conflict of interest under the Act are identified in Section 87103. With respect to
decisions relating to either the AZO or the MZO, Commissioner Fowle has the following interests,
based on the facts presented:

• A business interest in his agricultural business because he has an investment in that business
worth $2,000 or more (see Section 87103(a)), or holds a management position with, or is a
director, officer, partner, trustee, or employee of, that business (see Section 87103(d)).

• A real property interest in each of his RR/AG Parcels of real property because he has an
interest in each of those parcels worth $2,000 or more. (See Section 87103(b).)

• A source of income interest in his agricultural business because he has aggregated $500 or
more in income from that business in the past 12 months. (See Section 87103(c).)

• An interest in his personal finances. (See Section 87103.) An official always has an interest
in his or her personal finances and those of immediate family members.

Foreseeability and Materiality

Regulation 18701(a) provides that a decision's financial effect on an official's financial
interest is presumed to be reasonably foreseeable if the official's interest is "explicitly involved" in
the decision; an official's interest is "explicitly involved" if the interest is a named party in, or the
subject of, the decision; and an interest is the "subject of a proceeding" if the decision involves the

File No. A-19-067
Page No. 4

issuance, renewal, approval, denial or revocation of any license, permit, or other entitlement to, or
contract with, the interest. A property interest is explicitly involved in any decision affecting the
property as described in Regulation 18702.2(a)-(6). Regulation 18701(b) sets forth the
foreseeability standard applicable to a decision's effect on an official's interest that is not explicitly
involved in the decision, and provides that the decision's effect on such an interest is reasonably
foreseeable if it "can be recognized as a realistic possibility and more than hypothetical or
theoretical."

Regulation 18702.1 provides the materiality standards applicable to a decision's reasonably
foreseeable financial effect on an official's business interest, Regulation 18702.2 provides the
materiality standards applicable to a decision's reasonably foreseeable financial effect on an
official's property interest, and Regulation 18702.3 sets forth the materiality standards applicable to
a decision's reasonably foreseeable financial effect on an official's source of income interest. The
Fair Political Practices Commission repealed the former versions of Regulations 18702.1 and
18702.3, and adopted new versions of those regulations, at its regular meeting on July 23, 2019.
The analyses below apply those regulations as updated.

The Commissioner's Agricultural Business

Regulation 18702.1(a)(2) provides that a decision's effect on an official's business interest
is material if it may result in an increase or decrease of the business's annual gross revenues, or the
value of the business's assets or liabilities, in an amount equal to or more than five percent of the
business's annual gross revenues and no less than $10,000.² The Commissioner's agricultural
business's annual gross revenues are conservatively estimated at $530,000. Therefore, a decision
relating to the AZO, or a decision relating to the MZO, would have a material financial effect on the
Commissioner's business if that decision may result in an increase or decrease of the business's
annual gross revenues, or the value of its assets or liabilities, in an amount equal to or more than
$26,500.

You state that it is theoretically possible that the Commissioner's agricultural business could
achieve an additional five percent of its current annual gross income through the addition of an
agritourist use on his RR/AG Parcels pursuant to the AZO if enacted. The Commissioner's business

² Regulation 18702.1(a)(4) provides that a decision's reasonably foreseeable financial effect on an official's
business interest is material if the official knows or has reason to know that the business has an interest in real property
and there is clear and convincing evidence the decision would have a substantial effect on that interest. Because the
Commissioner also has real property interests in each of his RR/AG Parcels separate from his business real property
interests in those parcels, we apply Regulation 18702.2 to determine whether decisions relating to the MZO would have
a disqualifying effect on the Commissioner's real property interest in his RR/AG Parcels.

Regulation 18702.3 sets forth the Act's materiality standards applicable to a decision's reasonably foreseeable
effect on an official's source of income interest, and subdivision (a)(4) provides that a decision's effect on such an
interest is material if the source is a business that will be financially affected under the materiality standards of
Regulation 18702.1. Regulation 18702.5 sets forth the materiality standard applicable to a decision's reasonably
foreseeable financial effect on an official's interest in his or her personal finances, and subdivision (c) of that regulation
provides that if the decision at issue affects the official's interest in a business or a real property, materiality is
determined pursuant to Regulation 18702.1 or 18702.2. Therefore, we apply Regulations 18702.1 or
18702.2 to determine whether decisions relating to either the AZO or MZO would have a disqualifying effect on the
Commissioner's interests at issue.

File No. A-19-067
Page No. 5

already provides overnight stays on the Commissioner's RR/AG Parcels associated with the
business's on-site horse-training activities. Because decisions relating to the AZO may result in an
increase or decrease of the Commissioner's business's annual gross revenues of five percent or
more based on the facts presented, it is reasonably foreseeable that those decisions would have a
material financial effect on the Commissioner's business under Regulation 18702.1(a)(2). Thus, the
Commissioner is disqualified from taking part in decisions relating to the AZO based on the facts
presented.

While the Commissioner's RR/AG Parcels are theoretically able to accommodate a hog or
poultry pasture grazing operation, the transition to such an operation from his existing agricultural
business would be a major change. Furthermore, the facts presented provide no indication that the
Commissioner's business intends to establish a commercial hog or poultry pasture grazing
operation on the Commissioner's RR/AG Parcels or that such a use would be more profitable than
the Commissioner's existing use of the property. Because there is no indication that decisions
relating to the MZO would have an effect on the Commissioner's agricultural business worth at
least $26,500 (or five percent of the business's conservatively estimated annual gross revenues)
based on the facts presented, those decisions' effect on his interest in that business would not be
material.

The Commissioner's RR/AG Parcels

Regulation 18702.2(a)(2) provides that a decision's effect on an official's real property
interest is material if it determines the parcel's zoning or rezoning, other than a zoning decision
applicable to all properties designated in that category.

As described, the AZO is a zoning decision that determines the zoning rules for certain
agricultural properties. Moreover, it is not a zoning decision applicable to all properties designated
in a specific category because it applies only to RR/AG properties of 10 acres or more.
Accordingly, it is reasonably foreseeable that the AZO decisions will have a material effect on the
Commissioner's real property interest under Regulations 18701(a) and 18702.2(a)(2).

The MZO applies to all RR/AG Parcels within the County. In other words, the Ordinance
would authorize both youth agricultural projects and actively managed pasture grazing of hogs or
poultry on all parcels designated Rural Residential, Prime Agricultural, or Non-Prime Agricultural
within the County. Therefore, the effect of decisions relating to the MZO on the Commissioner's
real property interests in his RR/AG Parcels would not be material under Regulation 18702.2(a)(2)
based on the facts presented.³

Thus, the Act prohibits the Commissioner from taking part in decisions relating to the AZO
unless the Public Generally Exception or the Legally Required Participation Exception applies, but
does not prohibit him from taking part in decisions relating to the MZO.

³ You conservatively estimate, however, that the MZO would result in a slight increase in the market value of
the Commissioner's RR/AG Parcels due to the Ordinance's authorization of additional uses of those parcels. However,
because the zoning change applies to all the properties within the designated zoning category, the financial effect is not
considered disqualifying pursuant to Regulation 18702.2(a)(2).

File No. A-19-067
Page No. 6

Public Generally Exception

Regulation 18703 sets forth the Public Generally Exception, and subdivision (a) of that regulation provides as follows:

(a) General Rule. A governmental decision's financial effect on a public official's financial interest is indistinguishable from its effect on the public generally if the official establishes that a significant segment of the public is affected and the effect on his or her financial interest is not unique compared to the effect on the significant segment.

A significant segment of the public, as pertinent to your facts, is at least 25 percent of "[a]ll real property, commercial real property or residential real property within the official's jurisdiction." (Regulation 18703(b)(2).)

You have indicated that the AZO would apply to 1,734 parcels and the MZO would apply to 2,457 parcels. There are 57,183 parcels in the County. Based upon these numbers, it does not appear that the 25 percent minimum significant segment will be affected by the decision. Accordingly, we do not address the public generally exception further.

Legally Required Participation Exception

Under the Legally Required Participation Exception, set forth in Section 87101 and Regulation 18705, an official may make or participate in the making of a decision despite being financially interested in the decision if the official's participation is legally required for the action or decision to be made. A disqualified official may invoke the Exception only if there is no alternative source of decision consistent with the purposes and terms of the statute authorizing the decision. (Regulation 18705(a).)

However, the Legally Required Participation Exception is interpreted narrowly and cannot be invoked to allow a financially interested official to participate in a decision if a quorum can be convened of other members of the agency who are not disqualified. (Regulation 18705(c)(2).)

You note that the Planning Commission is made up of five members, and two Planning Commission members, other than the Commissioner, may also have disqualifying conflicts of interest with respect to decisions relating to the AZO. Because we have determined that the Commissioner has a disqualifying conflict of interest with respect to those decisions, the Planning Commission would not be able to form a quorum with respect to those decisions if the other two commissioners also have disqualifying conflicts of interest. Because there is no alternative source of decision with respect to the Planning Commission's decisions relating to the AZO, the Planning Commission may invoke the Exception with respect to those decisions, if it is unable to form a quorum of members who are not disqualified from those decisions under the Act's conflict of interest provisions.

If the Planning Commission invokes the Exception, only the smallest number of officials with disqualifying conflicts that are "legally required" may participate pursuant to the Exception; a "random means of selection" must be used to select only the number of otherwise disqualified

File No. A-19-067
Page No. 7

officials needed to form a quorum; and when an official is selected, he or she is selected for the duration of the proceedings in all related matters until his or her participation is no longer legally required, or the need for invoking the exception no longer exists. (Regulation 18705(c).)

If you have other questions on this matter, please contact me at (916) 322-5660.

Sincerely,

Dave Bainbridge
General Counsel

[signature]

By:     Brian G. Lau
        Assistant General Counsel

BGL:aja

(B) A proposed "Multispecies Zoning Ordinance" which would authorize "youth

sections.conclusion:
(1 )(A) Yes. The Act prohibits the Commissioner from taking part in decisions relating to the 
Agritourism Zoning Ordinance because it is reasonably foreseeable that those decisions would have 
a material financial effect on the Commissioner's interest in his agricultural business based on the 
facts presented. 
(1 )(B) No. The Act would not prohibit the Commissioner from taking part in decisions 
relating to the Multispecies Zoning Ordinance because it is not reasonably foreseeable that those 
decisions would have a material financial effect on the value of one or more of the Commissioner's 
parcels zoned Prime Agricultural or Non-Prime Agricultural based on the facts presented. 
(2) Yes. The Planning Commission may invoke the Legally Required Participation 
Exception in order to take action on the Agritourism Zoning Ordinance if it is unable to form a 
quorum of members who are not disqualified from those decisions.

Regulation 18702.2(a)(2) provides that a decision's effect on an official's real property 
interest is material if it determines the parcel's zoning or rezoning, other than a zoning decision 
applicable to all properties designated in that category. 
As described, the AZO is a zoning decision that determines the zoning rules for certain

in a specific category because it applies only to RR/AG properties,of 10 acres or more. 
Accordingly, it is reasonably foreseeable that the AZO decisions will have a material effect on the 
Commissioner's real property interest under Regulations 18701(a) and 18702.2(a)(2). 
The MZO applies to all RR/ AG Parcels within the County. In other words, the Ordinance 
would authorize both youth agricultural projects and actively managed pasture grazing of hogs or 
poultry on all parcels designated Rural Residential, Prime Agricultural, or Non-Prime Agricultural 
within the County. Therefore, the effect of decisions relating to the MZO on the Commissioner's 
real property interests in his RR/AG Parcels would not be material under Regulation 18702.2(a)(2) 
based on the facts presented. 3 
Thus, the Act prohibits the Commissioner from taking part in decisions relating to the AZO 
unless the Public Generally Exception or the Legally Required Participation Exception applies, but 
does not prohibit him from taking part in decisions relating to the MZO. 
3 You conservatively estimate, however, that the MZO would result in a slight increase in the market value of

because the zoning change applies to all the properties within the designated zoning category, the financial effect is not

Public Generally Exception  
Regulation 18703 sets forth the Public Generally Exception, and subdivision (a) of that 
regulation provides as follows:

official's financial interest is indistinguishable from its effect on the public 
generally if the official establishes that a significant segment of the public is 
affected and the effect on his or her financial interest is not unique compared 
to the effect on the significant segment. 
A significant segment of the public, as pertinent to your facts, is at least 25 percent of "[a]ll 
real property, commercial real property, or residential real property within the official's 
jurisdiction." (Regulation 18703(b )(2).) 
You have indicated that the AZO would apply to 1,734 parcels and the MZO would apply to 
2,457 parcels. There are 57,183 parcels in the County. Based upon these numbers, it does not 
appear that the 25 percent minimum significant segment will be affected by the decision. 
Accordingly, we do not address the public generally exception further. 
Legally Required Participa6on Exception 
Under the Legally Required Participation Exception, set forth in Section 87101 and 
Regulation 18705, an official may make or participate in the making of a decision despite being 
financially interested in the decision if the official's participation is legally required for the action or 
decision to be made. A disqualified official may invoke the Exception only if there is no alternative 
source of decision consistent with the purposes and terms of the statute authorizing the decision. 
(Regulation 18705(a).) 
However, the Legally Required Participation Exception is interpreted narrowly and cannot 
be invoked to allow a financially interested official to participate in a decision if a quorum can be 
convened of other members of the agency who are not disqualified. (Regulation 18705(c)(2).) 
You note that the Pianning Commission is made up of five members, and two Planning 
Commission members, other than the Commissioner, may also have disqualifying conflicts of 
interest with respect to decisions relating to the AZO. Because we have determined that the 
Commissioner has a disqualifying conflict of interest with respect to those decisions, the Planning 
Commission would not be able to form a quorum with respect to those decisions if the other two 
commissioners also have disqualifying conflicts of interest. Because there is no alternative source 
of decision with respect to the Planning Commission's decisions relating to the AZO, the Planning 
Commission may invoke the Exception with respect to those decisions, if it is unable to form a 
quorum of members who are not disqualified from those decisions under the Act's conflict of 
interest provisions. 
If the Planning Commission invokes the Exception, only the smallest number of officials 
with disqualifying conflicts that are "legally required" may participate pursuant to the Exception; a 
"random means of selection" must be used to select only the number of otherwise disqualified  
officials needed to form a quorum; and when an official is selected, he or she is selected for the 
duration of the proceedings in all related matters until his or her participati9n is no longer legally 
required, or the need for invoking the exception no longer exists. (Regulation 18705(c).)

embedding.qa_text:
QUESTION: (1) Does the Act prohibit Commissioner Fowle from taking part in governmental decisions 
relating to: 
(A) A proposed "Agritourism Zoning Ordinance," which would expressly authorize 
additional uses of real property parcels of 10 acres or more zoned Rural Residential, Prime 
Agricultural, or Non-Prime Agricultural given that the Commissioner's agricultural business 
is currently engaged in some of those uses on the Commissioner's real property zoned Prime 
Agricultural or Non-Prime Agricultural? 
(B) A proposed "Multispecies Zoning Ordinance" which would authorize "youth 
agricultural projects" and "actively managed pasture grazing of hogs or poultry" on real 
property zoned Rural Residential, Prime Agricultural, or Non-Prime Agricultural?(2) If the Commissioner is disqualified from decisions relating to either the Agritourism 
Zoning Ordinance or Multispecies Zoning Ordinance under the Act, may the Planning Commission 
invoke the Legally Required Participation Exception in order to take action on the Ordinance?

CONCLUSION: (1 )(A) Yes. The Act prohibits the Commissioner from taking part in decisions relating to the 
Agritourism Zoning Ordinance because it is reasonably foreseeable that those decisions would have 
a material financial effect on the Commissioner's interest in his agricultural business based on the 
facts presented. 
(1 )(B) No. The Act would not prohibit the Commissioner from taking part in decisions 
relating to the Multispecies Zoning Ordinance because it is not reasonably foreseeable that those 
decisions would have a material financial effect on the value of one or more of the Commissioner's 
parcels zoned Prime Agricultural or Non-Prime Agricultural based on the facts presented. 
(2) Yes. The Planning Commission may invoke the Legally Required Participation 
Exception in order to take action on the Agritourism Zoning Ordinance if it is unable to form a 
quorum of members who are not disqualified from those decisions.

Carroll Deputy County Counsel Office of the County Counsel County of Siskiyou P.O. Box 659 Yreka, CA 96097 Re: Your Request for Advice Our File No. A-19-067 Dear Mr. Carroll: This letter responds to your request for advice on behalf of Siskiyou County Planning Commissioner Jeff Fowle regarding the conflict of interest provisions of the Political Reform Act (the "Act").¹ Please note that we are only providing advice under the conflict of interest provisions of the Act and not under any other conflict of interest laws. Also note that we are not a finder of fact when considering advice (In re Oglesby (1975) 1 FPPC Ops. 71), and any advice we provide assumes your facts are complete and accurate. If this is not the case or if the facts underlying these decisions should change, you should contact us for additional advice. QUESTIONS (1) Does the Act prohibit Commissioner Fowle from taking part in governmental decisions relating to: (A) A proposed "Agritourism Zoning Ordinance," which would expressly authorize additional uses of real property parcels of 10 acres or more zoned Rural Residential, Prime Agricultural, or Non-Prime Agricultural given that the Commissioner's agricultural business is currently engaged in some of those uses on the Commissioner's real property zoned Prime Agricultural or Non-Prime Agricultural? (B) A proposed "Multispecies Zoning Ordinance" which would authorize "youth agricultural projects" and "actively managed pasture grazing of hogs or poultry" on real property zoned Rural Residential, Prime Agricultural, or Non-Prime Agricultural? ¹ The Political Reform Act is contained in Government Code Sections 81000 through 91014. All statutory references are to the Government Code, unless otherwise indicated. The regulations of the Fair Political Practices Commission are contained in Sections 18110 through 18997 of Title 2 of the California Code of Regulations. All regulatory references are to Title 2, Division 6 of the California Code of Regulations, unless otherwise indicated. File No. A-19-067 Page No. 2 (2) If the Commissioner is disqualified from decisions relating to either the Agritourism Zoning Ordinance or Multispecies Zoning Ordinance under the Act, may the Planning Commission invoke the Legally Required Participation Exception in order to take action on the Ordinance? CONCLUSIONS (1)(A) Yes. The Act prohibits the Commissioner from taking part in decisions relating to the Agritourism Zoning Ordinance because it is reasonably foreseeable that those decisions would have a material financial effect on the Commissioner's interest in his agricultural business based on the facts presented. (1)(B) No. The Act would not prohibit the Commissioner from taking part in decisions relating to the Multispecies Zoning Ordinance because it is not reasonably foreseeable that those decisions would have a material financial effect on the value of one or more of the Commissioner's parcels zoned Prime Agricultural or Non-Prime Agricultural based on the facts presented.
